title: The structure of a melt: The case of liquid bismuth
authors: Flor B. Quiroga, Isaías Rodríguez, David Hinojosa, Alexander Valladares, Renela M. Valladares, Ariel A. Valladares
abstract: Molecular Dynamics (MD) is performed on supercells of 216 atoms of bismuth, going from 300 K to 573 K in 100 steps and maintaining it in the liquid state, at 573 K, during 500 steps using the Materials Studio (MS) suite of codes. The Pair Distribution Functions (PDFs) and the Plane Angle Distributions (PADs) of the last 1, 10, 25 and 100 steps of the MD have been obtained. Averaging the last 100 steps, as representative of the liquid, Reverse Monte Carlo (RMC) was applied to obtain 4 atomic structures, one for each set of initial random velocities. Then, a detailed structural study of liquid bismuth at 573 K was undertaken; PDFs and PADs are calculated and reported. Two noticeable peaks appear for the PDFs, at 3.25 and 6.55 Å, along with a pseudo peak (shoulder) at 4.6 Å. This shoulder (after the first peak) of the PDFs is found to be related to the third and fourth neighbor peaks of the crystalline Wyckoff structure and also to the diagonal distances in deformed squares in the liquid structure. For completeness J(r)s are also reported. Two prominent peaks in the MS PADs are observed: 53° and 85°; and two for the RMC PADs: 58° and 90°, suggesting the existence of deformed triangles and squares. Less abundant are higher-order geometrical structures.
summary: This paper uses ab initio molecular dynamics and reverse Monte Carlo simulations to resolve a debate regarding the structural features of liquid bismuth. It identifies that the characteristic 'shoulder' in the pair distribution function is a real physical feature arising from deformed triangular and square atomic arrangements. The study provides a highly detailed geometric map of the liquid phase at 573 K.
main_problem: The study investigates the precise atomic structure of liquid bismuth at 573 K, specifically addressing whether the observed 'shoulder' in the Pair Distribution Function (PDF) is a physical structural feature or an experimental artifact.
main_result: The researchers conclude that the shoulder is a real physical property caused by the presence of deformed triangular and square atomic motifs. They also demonstrate that the liquid structure is statistically stable and that RMC-generated structures accurately represent the MD-simulated liquid.
method: Ab initio Molecular Dynamics (MD) using Density Functional Theory (DFT) with the LDA-VWN functional, combined with Reverse Monte Carlo (RMC) simulations to generate representative atomic supercells.
model_or_system: The physical system is liquid bismuth (Bi) at 573 K, modeled using supercells of 216 atoms transitioning from a crystalline Wyckoff structure to a metallic liquid state.
key_observables: Pair Distribution Functions (PDFs), Plane Angle Distributions (PADs), and Radial Distribution Functions (J(r)).
important_parameters: Temperature of 573 K, 216-atom supercells, LDA-VWN exchange-correlation functional, and a bond length threshold of 4.185 A.
main_assumptions: The use of the LDA-VWN functional and semi-core pseudopotentials as an approximation for the electronic structure; the assumption that the averaged MD steps represent a stable liquid state.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares Bi crystal patterns to geometric reliefs; Figure 2 shows the Bi phase diagram; Figure 3 shows energy stability; Figure 4 shows PDF convergence; Figure 5 compares MS and RMC PDFs; Figure 6 compares results to experimental data; Figures 7 and 8 show convergence of J(r) and PDFs; Figure 9 shows PAD peaks.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the structural problem of liquid Bi, describes the MD and RMC methodology, presents the convergence of the liquid state, analyzes the PDF and PAD features to identify geometric motifs, and concludes by comparing findings with experimental literature.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Comparison of complex crystalline and cultural structures. a) Bi crystal showing the colorful pattern and the geometrical hauts-reliefs of the mineral. b) Detail of the “Casa del Gobernador” façade in Uxmal, Yucatán, Mexico showing the hauts-reliefs. (Original image by Gary Todd, via Wikimedia Commons).

Figure 2. Phases of solid bismuth with the corresponding crystal structures. The insets are the crystalline structures observed. For a description of the phases see text. This diagram is a modification of the one in Ref [28].

Figure 3. Energy variation in electron-volts during the last 500 steps of the simulation. It can be seen that the slope of the line is -3.2 x 10-5, eV/step, with an uncertainty of 5 x 10-6 eV/step. The variation is negligible.

Figure 4. Comparison of the PDFs generated by MS with four different initial random velocities, showing a) the last step, b) the average of the last 10 steps, c) the average of the last 25 steps, and d) the average of the last 100 steps. In figure 4d) the PDFs are indistinguishable.

Figure 5 Comparison between the averages of the PDFs obtained from MS for 4 different velocities (dashed curves) and those from RMC. a) The averages of RMC and MS are also included. The curves are displaced vertically for a better appreciation; b) Curves superimposed; the similarity is evident.
